contraventionDescription: Stopped where prohibited (on a red route or clearway) Contravention code 46

sections.heading: How to have your penalty charge notice (PCN) for this parking contravention cancelled.
sections.content: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.
sections.contentHtml: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.

sections.heading: Follow our 3 stage process:
sections.content:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.
2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.
3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.
sections.contentHtml:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.

2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.

3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.

sections.heading: Specific grounds of appeal for this contravention
sections.content:
1.) There have been many appeals upheld on red routes where a motorist contended that they were stopped where prohibited in order to read the signs. The signage on red routes is confusing and often missing or facing the wrong way or the sheer number of different types of restriction make it too confusing. If you have genuinely stopped in order to work out whether you can actually stop then your appeal should be upheld. It maybe that you were stopped near a sign?
2.) If the Penalty Charge Notice (PCN) was issued just before restrictions ended or just after they started by around 2 minutes your PCN maybe cancelled if the issuing authority cannot provide evidence that the traffic wardens time piece is accurate. See adjudicators case below. Also most enforcing authorities have a set of guidelines issued to their enforcement officers. Usually these would state what grace period there is at the beginning and end of a controlled period.
3.) A Penalty Charge Notice must state the reasons for issue. If it just refers to a contravention code without a description there must be a key so you can identify what offence you are alleged to have committed. If not the PCN is invalid.
4.) There must be clear road-side signage indicating parking or loading restrictions  on each section of red line(s) and each marked bay and within a maximum of 15 metres (3 car lengths) of where you actually stopped on the same stretch of red line(s) or marked parking bay. The 15 metre rule applies to each section of Red line so if the line is interrupted by a road junction then there must be signs on both sides. Where you have stopped in a marked bay with restrictions there must be a sign in each bay. If not the bay is unenforceable. Where bays join for example where a loading bay joins a parking bay there must be a sign either at the point where the bays meet with arrows in each direction or within 5 metres of the boundary point on each side. We notice that this is quite often not the case. Also were the signs visible, quite often they are facing the wrong way or defaced or obstructed by other signs or trees etc. Transport for London must use correct and visible signage on their Red Routes otherwise they are unenforceable.

Signs on Red Routes must be facing the traffic rather than parallel to the road. Otherwise you would have to stop to read them. An example of this is featured in the adjudicators decisions.

5.) Were the red lines or bay markings clearly painted and properly visible? (were they covered by leaves of faded or were the previous yellow lines bleeding through for example)   At the point where the red line ends is there a line at right angles to the end.  In order to comply with the signage regulations there should be a “T” at the end of each stretch of red line even if it adjoins a parking bay.  Also were other road markings visible at the point where you parked for example, were there markings from a previous parking bay visible? Or were the number of road markings confusing? Please note that a missing “T” in itself may not be sufficient ground for a successful appeal unless you were parked at the end of the line and there were confusing markings.
6.) There are minimum widths of the various bays. Quite often the widths are less than they should be. If the bays are not the correct width then they are not marked correctly and are unenforceable.
The minimum widths are:
Parking, motorcycles taxies and cars 1.8 m
Loading bays 2.4m
Disabled bays 2.7m
There is also a minimum length for disabled bays only of 6.6m
Bays that are less than the minimum width are usually unenforceable.
7.) Do all the details on the PCN correspond to your vehicle?  i.e. registration, time and date, sometimes the colour is incorrectly stated. Is the Parking Attendant’s number and signature on the PCN? If not your appeal may be upheld. For details of the information which a PCN must contain in order to be valid.
8.) Civil enforcement officers (CEO’S) (formerly parking attendants) must be wearing their uniform, which includes their hat and have their ID Number clearly visible, usually on their lapel.  If you see them issuing a PCN and this number is not clearly identifiable, the ticket may be invalid. They should also be standing next to your car (not hiding behind a tree on the other side of the road)! Photographic evidence can be helpful. Again this in itself may not be a good enough reason to have your PCN cancelled on its own.
9.) If you have received two pcn’s for the same contravention on the same day at the same location you should appeal. Only one PCN can be issued for the same contravention each day. Sometimes you may receive a PCN on your windscreen but then also one in the post from a camera as red routes can be enforced by camera.
10.) Do not forget that an enforcing authority is able to consider compassionate grounds for cancelling a PCN. If you have checked the other points above and below and the “statutory grounds for appeal” and they do not assist you then you should write to the enforcing authority citing any compassionate or indeed other grounds upon which you would like them to consider cancelling your PCN.
11.) Was the ticket placed on your windscreen?  For a PCN to be legally issued by a Civil enforcement officer it must either be placed on your windscreen or handed to the person in charge of the vehicle otherwise it cannot be enforced unless the CEO has started the process of issuing a ticket or was prevented from issuing it.  (Unless issued by a traffic enforcement camera) However new rules that came into force in 2008 state that the penalty charge notice does not need to be placed on your windscreen and can be sent by post if the civil enforcement officer has started the process of issuing the PCN! However these type of tickets are very difficult to enforce and many cases have been lost at adjudication as the local authority were unable to prove that a contravention had occurred. It seems to us that a civil enforcement officer could just tap any registration into his computer and we suspect that this is the reason why they are so difficult to enforce. Without pictures and other information such as that taken from a tax disc it is difficult to prove. Obviously it depends on what stage the issuing process is at. If pictures have been taken and the CEO is standing by your car and the tax disc details have been taken but you quickly drive off just before the PCN is placed on your windscreen then your chances are much reduced. These tickets are referred to as section 10 VDA (vehicle driven away)
12.) You may have read recently about penalty charge notices issued by some enforcing authorities being unenforceable because they do not show the “date of issue on the actual ticket. (rather than the tear off section) It is correct that they cannot enforce a pcn if the date of issue is not on the actual ticket. There have been many cases but one is Mr S Aldridge v City of Westminster 18th April 2006. The date of the contravention is not the same as the date of issue.
13.) Also have the enforcement authority delayed the procedure of enforcing the PCN unnecessarily. Local authorities and transport for London do have an obligation to act fairly and this includes avoiding unnecessary delays. Normally if there is a delay of over 6 months at any stage your PCN should be cancelled by an adjudicator.
14.) Civil enforcement officers are now issued with digital cameras or have digital cameras integrated into their hand held computers. We suspect that this is to both cut down on fraud and also to provide evidence in the event of a representation or an appeal being made. Westminster council in London issued over 100,000 less parking tickets in the year immediately after equipping their parking attendants with digital cameras. The photographic evidence can also assist a motorist – for example. Does it actually show that a contravention has occurred? Also does it show the parking ticket affixed to the windscreen. Some local authorities will send you a copy of the photograph on request or you can usually view it online.
15.) The 1689 Bill of Rights act was a result of the struggle between the Stuart Kings and the English people and Parliament. The act basically enshrines a citizen’s right to resist arbitrary fines without first being convicted of an offence in a court of law. The interesting thing is that it does not seem that the act has been repealed and therefore may still apply. A motorist who received congestion charge fines has cited the act to transport for London and has had at least one fine cancelled as a result! If it applies to congestion charge fines then it would also apply to parking fines however we hear that adjudicators have not been too impressed with this argument.
16.) Before an enforcing authority can commence the enforcement of a parking regulation they must go through a procedure and issue a traffic management/regulation order. (Traffic order within London). We hear that sometimes these contain discrepancies. For example it could be that the length of a red line is longer than that stated in the order or the controlled hours are different to that enforced or that there is no order in place or it has been lost by the council! You could ask for a copy and also for the articles and exemptions. You may well find something wrong in it. In the event that you appeal to the parking adjudicator service they will request a copy as the parking adjudicator will need to see this so he knows the regulations which are in place.
17.) Enforcing authorities will have a protocol stating their policy on enforcement of different offences. For example how long they will observe a vehicle for before issuing a ticket where loading is permitted or how much of a vehicle should be stopped within a yellow box and for how long before a penalty charge notice is issued etc.  Some local authorities such as Westminster council in London publish their protocol on the internet. Others may provide a copy on request. We do not know whether a request for a copy under the freedom of information act would succeed but we believe a local authority should be open and in any event how would you know whether the community support officer or enforcement officer acted within their remit without sight of this.  We would suggest that you check the website of the enforcing authority and if their protocol is not published request a copy.
18.) If the penalty charge notice has been sent to you via post because for example the alleged offence was captured by a traffic enforcement camera was it received by you within 28 days. If not it is unenforceable.
19.) Please remember you can cite compassionate grounds when writing a letter of representation to a local authority. In fact it used to be the case that Transport for London would usually cancel any PCN issued if it was a first time contravention.
Back to Parking
